title: Enhancing Reasoning Capabilities of LLMs via Principled Synthetic Logic Corpus
authors: Terufumi Morishita, Gaku Morio, Atsuki Yamaguchi, Yasuhiro Sogawa (Advanced AI Innovation Center, Hitachi; Atsuki Yamaguchi at The University of Sheffield)
venue: NeurIPS 2024 (per arXiv Comments field); arXiv v1 2024-11-19, v2 2024-12-23
claim: LLMs' poor reasoning stems partly from a lack of high-quality logical reasoning samples in pre-training, so the authors propose Additional Logic Training (ALT): fine-tuning LLMs on a large synthetic deductive-logic corpus built from systematic design principles. They show ALT on their new corpus, Formal Logic Deduction Diverse (FLD×2 / FLD²), substantially improves state-of-the-art LLMs (including LLaMA-3.1-8B/70B) on logical reasoning, math, coding, and NLI benchmarks, with gains up to 30 points on logic, 10 on math/coding, 5 on BBH, and 6 on NLI (Abstract; Section 1).
novelty: The paper's stated contribution is not a new evaluation benchmark but (1) a set of four systematic 'design principles' (DP1 Reasoning with Unknown Facts, DP2 Illogical Reasoning / negative distractor samples, DP3/3'/3'' Diverse Reasoning Rules — axioms + representative theorems + variable step count s=1–8, DP4 Diverse Linguistic Expressions) derived from symbolic logic theory (completeness theorem) plus prior empirical findings, unifying and extending the earlier FLD generator (Morishita et al. 2023); (2) the FLD×2 corpus itself, which scales vocabulary (~100k WordNet words vs ~15k in FLD), rule diversity (~50 axioms+theorems vs 13), and adds 'adversarial' (rather than random) distractor formulas and richer linguistic templates (Section 3, Table 1); and (3) demonstrating for the first time that this style of synthetic-logic fine-tuning still helps very large, heavily pre-trained state-of-the-art LLMs (LLaMA-3.1-70B, 15T+ tokens), not just small models like T5/RoBERTa/GPT-2 as in prior work (Section 4, Appendix A.2), plus a Recall-Adam knowledge-forgetting-prevention step shown to be necessary for the gains to hold (Section 5, Appendix F).

protocol.description: This is a supervised-fine-tuning (SFT) + downstream-benchmark-transfer study, not an in-context few-shot evaluation of the deduction task itself. LLMs are trained (ALT) directly on FLD×2 deduction samples (facts+hypothesis as input, logical-steps+answer-label as output, loss only on the output span) for 1 epoch on 100k samples (Section 4). The trained models are then evaluated on 31 separate downstream benchmarks (not FLD×2 itself) using 5-shot in-context learning (3-shot for BBH and AbductionRules) (Section 4, 'Benchmarks'). The paper explicitly excludes the synthetic logic corpora from its own evaluation set: 'we excluded the synthetic logic corpora used for training, as training on them often leads to overfitting to their superficial and statistical cues... failing to measure truly generalizable reasoning capabilities' (Section 4). So there is no reported single-shot or interactive accuracy number for models solving fresh FLD×2-generated proved/disproved/unknown instances directly — the paper's 'observation' unit at eval time is a 5-shot-demonstrated downstream benchmark item (e.g., FOLIO, LogicNLI, bAbI), not a raw FLD×2 theory+query pair.
protocol.observation_content: unverified — missing from extraction for the raw FLD×2 instance-as-eval-prompt case (the paper does not report evaluating models on freshly generated FLD×2 instances via direct prompting; it only trains on them). What IS specified: the training-time sample format is facts (premises) + hypothesis as input, with the model required to 'generate logical steps to derive a given hypothesis from provided facts' plus an answer label (Section 1 Fig. 2 caption; Appendix D.1). Appendix D.1 quote: '“proved” indicating that the hypothesis can be proved by the logical steps, “disproved” indicating that the hypothesis can be disproved, and “unknown” indicating that the given facts are insufficient for either proving or disproving the hypothesis. For samples with “unknown” labels, the logical steps are “None.”' At downstream-eval time, the model sees 5 (or 3) in-context demonstrations per benchmark item (5-shot ICL), i.e. a few-shot single forward-pass protocol per query, not a multi-turn/interactive loop with feedback.

representations: unverified — missing from extraction for direct accuracy-by-representation comparisons on FLD×2 test instances (none reported, since FLD×2 test split is excluded from evaluation, Section 4). The paper does report a corpus-design-variant ('representation dial') comparison: Table 1 compares FLD×2 against RuleTaker, PARARULE-Plus, and FLD on vocabulary size, distractor type (random vs. adversarial), number of deduction rules, logical steps range, and expressions-per-formula; Table 2 shows downstream-benchmark accuracy after ALT on each of these corpora (ALT-FLD×2 highest average, 52.0, vs ALT-FLD 51.9, ALT-RT 50.1, ALT-PRP 48.1, base LLaMA-3.1-8B 47.9 — Table 2). Table 3 is an ablation of FLD×2 itself removing one design principle at a time (w/o DP1/DP2/DP3.rules/DP3.steps/DP4), showing every ablation degrades the average score relative to full FLD×2 (52.0 → 50.6–51.4 depending on which DP is removed) (Section 5, Table 3).

models.name: LLaMA-3.1-8B
strategies: supervised fine-tuning (ALT) on synthetic logic corpus
5-shot in-context learning at downstream eval (3-shot for BBH/AbductionRules)
Recall Adam optimizer for knowledge-forgetting prevention during ALT
models.key_results: Base LLaMA-3.1-8B average across 31 benchmarks = 47.9; +ALT-FLD×2 = 52.0 (best among corpora tested); +ALT-FLD = 51.9; +ALT-RT (RuleTaker) = 50.1; +ALT-PRP (PARARULE-Plus) = 48.1 (Table 2, Section 5). Ablating any single design principle from FLD×2 degrades the 8B average (w/o DP1=51.4, w/o DP2=50.6, w/o DP3.rules=50.7, w/o DP3.steps=51.1, w/o DP4=51.3) vs full FLD×2=52.0 (Table 3).

models.name: LLaMA-3.1-70B
strategies: 5-shot in-context learning at downstream eval (3-shot for BBH/AbductionRules)
models.key_results: Benchmark-wise 5-shot performance before/after ALT-FLD×2 (Table 4): FOLIO 58.9→66.7; LogicNLI 34.9→50.9; RobustLR 49.6→81.6; LogiQA 64.3→69.4; AbductionRules 84.0→95.7; bAbiD roughly flat (83.8→83.5); AR-LSAT 21.5→25.0; ReClor roughly flat (33.7→36.3); ART roughly flat (85.4→85.5). Aggregate claims: 'up to 30 points' gain on logical reasoning tasks (Fig. 2c, Section 1/6.1), 'up to 7 and 10 points' on math and coding respectively (Fig. 2d/2e, Section 6.2), 'up to 6 points' on NLI (Fig. 2f, Section 6.3), and 'up to 2 points' on other/mixed tasks (Fig. 2g, Section 6.4). Without Recall Adam (knowledge-forgetting prevention), ALT-trained models underperformed even the no-ALT baseline (Appendix F, Table F.8; qualitative claim only in extraction).

headline_numbers: Abstract: gains of 'up to 30 points on logical reasoning benchmarks, up to 10 points on math and coding benchmarks, and 5 points on the benchmark suite BBH.'
Table 2: 5-shot average across 31 benchmarks for LLaMA-3.1-8B: base 47.9 → +ALT-FLD×2 52.0 (largest gain among 4 corpora tested: FLD 51.9, RuleTaker 50.1, PARARULE-Plus 48.1).
Table 3 (ablation): removing any one of DP1/DP2/DP3.rules/DP3.steps/DP4 from FLD×2 lowers the 8B average from 52.0 to between 50.6 and 51.4, i.e., every design principle contributes measurably.
Table 4: LLaMA-3.1-70B FOLIO 58.9→66.7, LogicNLI 34.9→50.9, RobustLR 49.6→81.6, AbductionRules 84.0→95.7 after ALT-FLD×2.

Appendix D.2: FLD×2 corpus size = 100k/5k/5k train/valid/test samples; depth s=1–8 logical steps; ~50 axioms+theorems; ~100k-word WordNet-derived vocabulary; 0–20 distractors per sample (Section 3, DP2 discussion).

Section 4: training used 100k samples for 1 epoch (~0.1B tokens), batch size 256 (390 steps), lr 2e-05 (8B) / 3e-06 (70B); compute = ~1 week x 128 H100 GPUs total including preliminary experiments (Appendix E.3).
failure_modes: Table 6 gives concrete post-ALT failures for LLaMA-3.1-70B: (1) ARC-challenge items that are pure knowledge-recall (photosynthesis step order) — ALT does not teach new facts so these remain unimproved; (2) GPQA items requiring domain knowledge (quantum mechanics expectation value) beyond what reasoning-only training can supply; (3) ARC-challenge multiple-choice items requiring a complex multi-step procedure (reason about each option separately, then select the single best answer under subtle contextual nuance) — the paper concludes 'this complex procedure contrasts with logical reasoning and NLI problems, where LLMs can directly obtain an answer from a single reasoning trace... ALT alone does not teach LLMs such task-specific procedures' (Section 6.4). Separately, Section 2.2 (Design Principle 2 motivation) reports a training-time failure mode observed in preliminary experiments: an LLM trained only on valid modus-ponens-with-conjunction samples would still hallucinate the conclusion (ℋ) even when premises were logically insufficient (Eq. 8) — 'an LLM could output ℋ, which was indeed often observed in our preliminary experiments' — motivating the addition of explicit negative/insufficient-premise distractor samples. Also, Appendix F reports that omitting the Recall Adam knowledge-forgetting-prevention step during ALT causes trained models to underperform even the no-ALT baseline, attributed to unknown-fact training samples displacing the LLM's pretrained factual knowledge (Section 5).
one_shot_vs_multistep: The paper's own framing is not single-shot-vs-interactive-loop but train-once-then-transfer: ALT is a single fine-tuning pass over synthetic logic samples (facts+hypothesis → logical-steps+label, loss only on outputs, Section 4), after which the model is evaluated with few-shot (5-shot, or 3-shot for BBH/AbductionRules) in-context prompting on each downstream benchmark — there is no multi-turn interactive refinement loop or verifier feedback in the eval protocol described. The paper does note within-sample multi-step structure matters: 'empirical evidence has shown that LLMs struggle with constructing multi-step deductive reasoning with large steps s' (Section 2.3, citing Gontier et al. 2020 and Morishita et al. 2023), which is why FLD×2 also folds in single-step 'theorems' (syllogism, contraposition, De Morgan's) as a countermeasure (Design Principle 3''), rather than relying solely on chained multi-step derivations from atomic axioms. Table 6's third failure case additionally illustrates that even after ALT, correctly handling a task that requires generating and then comparing several independent single-pass reasoning traces (as in multi-choice QA) remains an unsolved 'complex procedure' distinct from ALT's single deductive-trace training signal (Section 6.4).

relation_to_candidate.position: This paper sits upstream of c18 on the family tree as a corpus-generation and training-methodology paper, not an inference-time evaluation paper. Its protocol axis is single-pass supervised fine-tuning (one gradient-updating training run over many synthetic instances) followed by few-shot (5-shot) single-forward-pass evaluation on unrelated downstream benchmarks — it never scores a model's one-shot or interactive accuracy on freshly generated FLD×2 theories themselves (those are explicitly excluded from eval, Section 4). Where it is observed: downstream benchmark items via few-shot ICL, not FLD×2 instances. Output shape at training time is the full intermediate proof (logical steps) plus a final proved/disproved/unknown label (Appendix D.1, Fig. 2), i.e., structured full-sequence output, not a bare label. Verifier type: the label plus proof-steps are generated by the deterministic sample generator itself (forward/backward random deduction over axioms, Appendix D.3) and are definitionally correct by construction, not checked by an independent prover at training or eval time.
relation_to_candidate.overlap: Shares the deep structural DNA c18 draws on: fictional-content-agnostic facts+if-then-rules theories, a proved/disproved/unknown (i.e., True/False/Unknown-equivalent) three-way label, explicit controllable proof depth (s=1–8 logical steps), and explicit distractor control (0–20 distractors per sample, with 'adversarial' distractors specifically designed to be insufficient-premise near-misses) as the latent difficulty dial — both match c18's description of 'depth bins and rule/distractor composition are the latent difficulty dials.' Both also rely on synthetic/fresh generation to avoid contamination, though this paper frames that benefit as enabling 'unknown facts'/arbitrary content (Design Principle 1) rather than explicitly as anti-contamination for eval integrity.
relation_to_candidate.differences: Critical differences: (1) this paper's task is deduction/training, not single-call evaluation — it measures transfer-benchmark accuracy after fine-tuning, never reports direct model accuracy at labeling fresh FLD×2 instances via prompting alone (single-shot or few-shot), so it has essentially no directly reusable number for 'how well can an LLM answer a depth-d FLD×2 query out of the box'; (2) the output the paper cares about includes full intermediate logical steps (a proof), whereas c18 deliberately drops full proof-chain generation from its own design ('Full proof-chain generation as output — dropped from the design; the adaptation keeps the label only, to bound output tokens and preserve exact-match scoring', c18.md Dead branches) — so this paper's core object of study (step generation quality) is exactly what c18 excludes; (3) content is WordNet-vocabulary-based ('Foo star', 'Bar star', abstract predicate letters converted via linguistic templates) rather than PrOntoQA-style fictional-but-natural-sounding ontology terms ('wumpus'/'yumpus') that c18 specifically inherits from PrOntoQA, though both achieve the same functional goal of unknown/arbitrary content; (4) FLD×2's distractors are 'adversarial' (missing-information near-misses engineered per Design Principle 2) rather than the CWA/negation-as-failure and open-world Unknown semantics c18 draws from ProofWriter; this paper does not appear to model open-world Unknown as a semantics distinct from 'insufficient premises for either proof or disproof' (Appendix D.1) — unverified whether it treats CWA vs. open-world as a further design axis beyond what's summarized in Appendix D.1.
relation_to_candidate.what_c18_would_add: c18 would add: (a) a genuine inference-time single-shot/few-shot evaluation protocol scoring off-the-shelf (not fine-tuned) LLMs' T/F/Unknown exact-match accuracy directly on fresh depth-controlled instances — something this paper does not report at all, since it excludes its own corpus from evaluation; (b) an independent verifier (the c18 adaptation plans a ~30-50-line forward-chaining fixpoint oracle, per c18.md Implementation lens) rather than trusting the generator's definitional label, addressing the same soundness concern the c18 measurement-skeptic notes ('a soundness bug in generation would not be caught by the label alone'); (c) a PrOntoQA-flavored fictional-ontology surface (contamination-resistant nonce predicates) plus a CSP/seating-puzzle stratum, both absent from FLD×2's WordNet+template generation; (d) bounding output to the label alone (dropping step-by-step proof generation) to preserve clean exact-match scoring, in contrast to this paper's full logical-steps-plus-label output target.

corpus_claim: FLD* (Morishita et al., NeurIPS 2024) is the follow-up to FLD extending the same proved/disproved/unknown generator line.
corpus_claim_verdict: partially-supported / miscited-in-name. The arXiv paper at 2411.12498 (Morishita, Morio, Yamaguchi, Sogawa, NeurIPS 2024) IS the intended follow-up to FLD (Morishita et al. 2023) and DOES extend the same proved/disproved/unknown-label generator line under controllable depth (s=1–8) and distractors — this substantive part of the claim is well supported (Section 3, Table 1, Appendix D.1). However, the paper never uses the name 'FLD*' anywhere in its text; its own name for the corpus is 'Formal Logic Deduction Diverse,' stylized 'FLD×2' or 'FLD²' (Abstract, Section 1, Section 3). 'FLD*' appears to be a project-internal shorthand invented outside the paper, not the authors' own naming (see the paper's header 'Discrepancies vs. expected citation' note). Correction: refer to the corpus as FLD×2 (FLD²), not FLD*, when citing this paper; the underlying lineage/label-scheme claim otherwise holds.
baseline_relevant: Depth/difficulty dials used by the generator: logical steps s ranges 1–8 (Section 2.3 DP3', Section 3 DP3-3'', Table 1); ~50 deduction rules total (13 axioms + ~37 representative theorems such as modus ponens, syllogism, contraposition, De Morgan's laws) vs. only 13 in the original FLD (Section 3, DP3-3'').

Distractor dial: 0 to 20 distractors per sample, randomly chosen, and distractors are 'adversarial' (missing-information near-misses, e.g., ℱ alone as a distractor for premise ℱ∧𝐀) rather than random formulas as in prior corpora (Section 3, DP2; Table 1).

Vocabulary/content dial: FLD×2 uses ~100k words from full WordNet vs. ~15k (WordNet subset) in FLD and ≤100 hand-selected vocabulary in RuleTaker/PARARULE-Plus (Table 1) — larger vocabulary is explicitly argued (DP1) to be necessary for the model to learn that predicate content is truly arbitrary/unknown.

Label scheme and format: three-way 'proved'/'disproved'/'unknown' label; for 'unknown' samples the logical-steps field is literally the string 'None.' (Appendix D.1) — directly relevant to how a baseline harness should structure ground truth for the Unknown case.

Corpus split sizes: 100k/5k/5k train/valid/test (Appendix D.2) — useful scale reference, though this paper does not report model accuracy on its own test split (it is excluded from evaluation to avoid overfitting-to-superficial-cues effects, Section 4).

Contamination/release status: corpus, code, and trained model are released 'under a permissive license' at https://github.com/hitachi-nlp/FLD (Section 1, footnote 1) — meaning FLD×2's exact released instances are public and therefore contamination-risked for any benchmark reuse; a baseline built on c18 should regenerate fresh instances rather than reuse the released FLD×2 split, consistent with c18's own design decision to refuse 'reuse of published RuleTaker/ProofWriter/PrOntoQA sets verbatim' (c18.md Dead branches).

No floor/ceiling numbers exist for raw model accuracy at solving FLD×2-style T/F/Unknown queries directly (single-shot or few-shot) — this is a genuine gap relative to what a c18 baseline needs; the paper's only quantitative signal is downstream-transfer accuracy after fine-tuning (Tables 2–4), not direct task accuracy.

Design-principle ablation (Table 3) gives a rough sensitivity ranking useful for prioritizing which difficulty/design dials matter most: removing DP2 (illogical/distractor samples) or DP3.rules (rule diversity) hurt the 8B average the most (52.0→50.6 and 52.0→50.7 respectively) versus DP1/DP4 (52.0→51.4/51.3), suggesting distractor design and rule diversity are the highest-leverage knobs, at least for this paper's fine-tuning-transfer setup (not necessarily for zero/few-shot prompting of a frozen LLM).
quotes: "we construct a synthetic corpus named Formal Logic D eduction D iverse (FLD ×2 ), comprising numerous samples of multi-step deduction with unknown facts, diverse reasoning rules, diverse linguistic expressions, and challenging distractors." (Abstract / Section 1)

"In addition to the logical steps, the samples of FLD ×2 and previous corpora include answer labels ( Figure D.3 ): “proved” indicating that the hypothesis can be proved by the logical steps, “disproved” indicating that the hypothesis can be disproved, and “unknown” indicating that the given facts are insufficient for either proving or disproving the hypothesis. For samples with “unknown” labels, the logical steps are “None.”" (Appendix D.1, Answer Labels)
"FLD ×2 includes 100k/5k/5k samples for train/valid/test splits." (Appendix D.2, Splits)

"We include samples with up to s = 8 logical steps, following (Morishita et al., 2023 ) ... we include a variable number of distractors in each sample, randomly chosen from a range of 0 to 20." (Section 3, DP2/DP3-3'' discussion)

"We evaluated the trained LLMs on 31 benchmarks shown in Table E.7 using 5-shot in-context learning, except for BBH and AbuductionRules, which used 3-shot in-context learning... we excluded the synthetic logic corpora used for training, as training on them often leads to overfitting to their superficial and statistical cues." (Section 4, Benchmarks)
"We observed a substantial improvement of up to 30 points on logical reasoning tasks ( Figure 2(c) )." (Section 1, Introduction)
"an LLM could output ℋ, which was indeed often observed in our preliminary experiments." (Section 2.2, Teaching Illogical Reasoning)
"We release the corpus, code, and the trained model under a permissive license https://github.com/hitachi-nlp/FLD ." (Section 1, Introduction)
